original_flashcards.front: Describe the reaction that occurs when bromine water is added to a solution of sodium iodide.
original_flashcards.back: Bromine, being more reactive than iodine, displaces the iodide ions. Bromine is reduced to bromide ions, and iodide ions are oxidised to iodine. The solution will turn brown (due to the formation of Iв‚‚).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 4: Inorganic Chemistry and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: Write the balanced ionic equation for the reaction between bromine and iodide ions.
original_flashcards.back:
Brв‚‚ + 2IвЃ» в†’ Iв‚‚ + 2BrвЃ» 

(The full equation is Brв‚‚ + 2NaI в†’ Iв‚‚ + 2NaBr)

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 4: Inorganic Chemistry and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: When testing for halide ions, what is the initial precipitate formed if both bromide and iodide ions are present in a solution, and silver nitrate is added?
original_flashcards.back: The precipitate will be a mixture of silver bromide (AgBr, cream precipitate) and silver iodide (AgI, yellow precipitate).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 4: Inorganic Chemistry and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: How does silver bromide (AgBr) behave when treated with dilute and concentrated aqueous ammonia?
original_flashcards.back: Silver bromide is sparingly soluble in dilute ammonia but dissolves readily in concentrated ammonia.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 4: Inorganic Chemistry and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: How does silver iodide (AgI) behave when treated with dilute and concentrated aqueous ammonia?
original_flashcards.back: Silver iodide is insoluble in both dilute and concentrated ammonia.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 4: Inorganic Chemistry and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: How does the oxidising strength of chlorine compare to that of fluorine?
original_flashcards.back:
Chlorine is a weaker oxidising agent than fluorine. 

(Remember: Oxidising strength decreases down Group 17 as atomic radius increases and electron affinity decreases).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 4: Inorganic Chemistry and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: Can chlorine oxidise fluoride ions? Explain why.
original_flashcards.back: No, chlorine cannot oxidise fluoride ions. This is because chlorine is a weaker oxidising agent than fluorine, meaning it is less able to gain electrons than fluorine. Fluoride ions (FвЃ») are very stable and difficult to oxidise.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: Which gas is typically identified using a glowing splint?
original_flashcards.back: Oxygen (Oв‚‚).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: What is the positive result for the test for oxygen gas using a glowing splint?
original_flashcards.back: The glowing splint relights.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What is the common name of the gas that is colourless and supports combustion?
original_flashcards.back: Oxygen (Oв‚‚).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Describe the appearance of oxygen gas.
original_flashcards.back: It is a colourless gas.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 4: Inorganic Chemistry and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: What are the products of the thermal decomposition of lithium nitrate?
original_flashcards.back:
Lithium oxide, nitrogen dioxide, and oxygen. 

(Note: Unlike other Group 1 nitrates which form nitrites and oxygen, lithium nitrate decomposes similarly to Group 2 nitrates due to the high charge density of the LiвЃє ion).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 4: Inorganic Chemistry and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: Write the balanced chemical equation for the thermal decomposition of lithium nitrate.
original_flashcards.back: 4LiNOв‚ѓ в†’ 2Liв‚‚O + 4NOв‚‚ + Oв‚‚

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: Describe an experimental setup to compare the thermal stability of metal hydroxides by collecting the water produced.
original_flashcards.back: Heat each hydroxide sample. Use a delivery tube to bubble the gas (water vapour) produced from each hydroxide into a flask or test tube cooled in an ice bath. This allows the water vapour to condense and be collected as a liquid.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: How can the collected water be used to compare the thermal stability of different metal hydroxides?
original_flashcards.back: You can compare the mass of water collected over a set time at a constant heating temperature. Alternatively, you could compare the time taken for a set mass loss from the hydroxide sample. A less thermally stable hydroxide will produce water more quickly or lose mass faster.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What is the initial colour of the solution in a titration of FeВІвЃє with MnOв‚„вЃ»?
original_flashcards.back: Pale green (due to FeВІвЃє ions).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What is the colour change observed at the endpoint of a titration of FeВІвЃє with MnOв‚„вЃ»?
original_flashcards.back: The solution turns purple/pink (due to the first permanent excess of MnOв‚„вЃ» ions).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Explain why ethanol is soluble in water.
original_flashcards.back: Ethanol is soluble in water because it can form hydrogen bonds with water molecules. The energy released from forming these new hydrogen bonds is comparable to or greater than the energy required to break the existing hydrogen bonds in pure ethanol and water.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Explain why ethanol is soluble in hexane.
original_flashcards.back: Ethanol is soluble in hexane because it can form London forces (induced dipole-induced dipole forces) with hexane molecules. The London forces between the non-polar alkyl chain of ethanol and hexane are similar in strength, allowing them to mix.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Explain why calcium fluoride (CaFв‚‚) is soluble in water.
original_flashcards.back: Calcium fluoride is soluble in water because its ions (CaВІвЃє and FвЃ») are hydrated by water molecules. The enthalpy change of hydration (energy released when ions are surrounded by water) is greater than or compensates for the lattice enthalpy (energy required to break apart the ionic lattice).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Explain why calcium fluoride (CaFв‚‚) is insoluble in hexane.
original_flashcards.back: Calcium fluoride is insoluble in hexane because hexane is a non-polar solvent. Any weak London forces that could form between hexane molecules and the CaВІвЃє and FвЃ» ions would be much smaller in magnitude than the strong ionic bonds within the calcium fluoride lattice, so the lattice cannot be overcome.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: What is observed when a blue solid, such as copper nitrate, is thermally decomposed? (Part 1)
original_flashcards.back: The blue solid turns black (due to the formation of copper(II) oxide, CuO).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: What other observations are typically made during the thermal decomposition of a metal nitrate like copper nitrate? (Part 2)
original_flashcards.back:
вЂў Condensation / colourless liquid (water) on the sides of the test tube.
вЂў Brown gas / brown fumes (nitrogen dioxide, NOв‚‚) produced.
вЂў (Less obvious) Greenish-yellow gas (oxygen, Oв‚‚) may also be produced.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What is the primary purpose of adding an indicator in a titration?
original_flashcards.back: To show the endpoint of the reaction.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What does the endpoint of a titration signify?
original_flashcards.back: The endpoint signifies when the reaction is complete, or when neutralisation has occurred (for acid-base titrations), usually marked by a significant change in pH.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What is the oxidation number of chlorine in its elemental form (Clв‚‚)?

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: In the reaction where chlorine disproportionates, what are the oxidation numbers of chlorine in the products sodium chloride (NaCl) and sodium chlorate(V) (NaClOв‚ѓ)?
original_flashcards.back:
вЂў In NaCl, chlorine has an oxidation number of -1.
вЂў In NaClOв‚ѓ, chlorine has an oxidation number of +5.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Explain why the reaction where chlorine forms NaCl and NaClOв‚ѓ is classified as a disproportionation reaction.
original_flashcards.back: It is a disproportionation reaction because chlorine (Clв‚‚) is simultaneously oxidised (from 0 to +5 in NaClOв‚ѓ) and reduced (from 0 to -1 in NaCl).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Compare the ionic radii of magnesium ions (MgВІвЃє) and calcium ions (CaВІвЃє).
original_flashcards.back: The magnesium ion (MgВІвЃє) has a smaller ionic radius than the calcium ion (CaВІвЃє).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 4: Inorganic Chemistry and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: How does the size of a Group 2 metal ion affect its polarising power?
original_flashcards.back: A smaller metal ion (like MgВІвЃє) has a higher charge density and thus a greater polarising power.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: Explain how the polarising power of MgВІвЃє affects the thermal stability of magnesium carbonate compared to calcium carbonate.
original_flashcards.back: The smaller MgВІвЃє ion has a greater polarising power, causing more distortion (polarisation) of the electron cloud of the carbonate ion (COв‚ѓВІвЃ»). This weakens the C-O bonds, making magnesium carbonate thermally decompose at a lower temperature than calcium carbonate.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 4: Inorganic Chemistry and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: What visible change occurs when chlorine gas is bubbled through a solution containing iodide ions?
original_flashcards.back: The colourless solution turns brown or yellow-brown, due to the formation of iodine (Iв‚‚).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 4: Inorganic Chemistry and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: Write the ionic equation for the reaction between chlorine gas and iodide ions.
original_flashcards.back: Clв‚‚(g) + 2IвЃ»(aq) в†’ Iв‚‚(aq) + 2ClвЃ»(aq)

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Which halide ion is typically associated with the formation of a cream precipitate with silver nitrate solution, which partially dissolves in dilute ammonia?
original_flashcards.back: Bromide ion (BrвЃ»).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 4: Inorganic Chemistry and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: Which halide ion is displaced by chlorine in an aqueous solution but not by iodine?
original_flashcards.back: Bromide ion (BrвЃ»).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What is the chemical formula for the aluminium ion?
original_flashcards.back: AlВівЃє

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What is the name of the ion with the formula AlВівЃє?
original_flashcards.back: Aluminium ion

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 4: Inorganic Chemistry and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: Write the ionic equation for the precipitation reaction between silver ions and chloride ions.
original_flashcards.back: AgвЃє(aq) + ClвЃ»(aq) в†’ AgCl(s)

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What type of reaction occurs when silver ions react with chloride ions, and what is observed?
original_flashcards.back: A precipitation reaction occurs, forming a white precipitate of silver chloride (AgCl).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table
board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: What is the general trend for first ionisation energy across a period in the periodic table?
original_flashcards.back: First ionisation energy generally increases across a period.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table
board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: Explain the primary reason for the increase in first ionisation energy across a period.
original_flashcards.back: Across a period, the nuclear charge (number of protons) increases, leading to a stronger attraction between the nucleus and the outer electrons.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table
board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: How does shielding affect the first ionisation energy across a period, and what is the overall consequence?
original_flashcards.back: Shielding remains relatively similar across a period because electrons are added to the same principal energy shell. This, combined with the increased nuclear charge, results in a stronger net attraction for the outer electrons, requiring more energy to remove them.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Why can Aluminium Chloride (AlClв‚ѓ) act as a Lewis acid?
original_flashcards.back: AlClв‚ѓ acts as a Lewis acid because the aluminium atom in AlClв‚ѓ has an incomplete octet (only 6 valence electrons) and can therefore accept a lone pair of electrons from another species.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What is the definition of a Lewis acid?
original_flashcards.back: A Lewis acid is defined as an electron pair acceptor.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: When preparing a solution in a volumetric flask, why is it important to invert the flask several times after making it up to the mark?
original_flashcards.back: To ensure thorough mixing of the solution, creating a homogeneous solution with a uniform concentration throughout.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 5: Formulae, Equations and Amounts of Substance

original_flashcards.front: What is the consequence of not adequately mixing a solution prepared in a volumetric flask?
original_flashcards.back: If not adequately mixed, the solution will not be homogeneous, meaning different parts of the solution will have different concentrations. This would lead to inaccurate results in subsequent experiments (e.g., titrations).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: State one important benefit of adding chlorine to public water supplies.
original_flashcards.back: Chlorine kills or destroys bacteria, microbes, and pathogens present in the water.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: How does the addition of chlorine to water supplies contribute to public health?
original_flashcards.back: By killing pathogens, chlorine prevents the spread of waterborne diseases, thereby making the water safe for human consumption and significantly improving public health.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Compare the thermal decomposition of calcium nitrate and strontium nitrate, explaining which decomposes at a lower temperature.
original_flashcards.back: Calcium nitrate, Ca(NOв‚ѓ)в‚‚, decomposes at a lower temperature than strontium nitrate, Sr(NOв‚ѓ)в‚‚.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Explain the role of ionic size and charge density in determining the thermal stability of Group 2 nitrates.
original_flashcards.back: The calcium ion (CaВІвЃє) is smaller than the strontium ion (SrВІвЃє). This means the CaВІвЃє ion has a higher charge density than the SrВІвЃє ion.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 4: Inorganic Chemistry and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: How does the higher charge density of a Group 2 cation affect the nitrate anion and its thermal decomposition?
original_flashcards.back: A higher charge density in the cation (e.g., CaВІвЃє) polarises the large nitrate anion (NOв‚ѓвЃ») more strongly. This polarisation weakens the nitrogen-oxygen bonds within the nitrate ion, requiring less energy to break and thus decomposing more readily at a lower temperature.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: Explain why the direct reaction between two negatively charged ions, such as chromate(VI) and iodide, is often slow.
original_flashcards.back: Both chromate(VI) ions (CrOв‚„ВІвЃ») and iodide ions (IвЃ») are negatively charged.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: What is the consequence of electrostatic repulsion between negatively charged reactants on the activation energy and reaction rate?
original_flashcards.back: The repulsion between negatively charged ions requires a high activation energy to overcome this electrostatic repulsion for them to react, leading to a slow reaction rate.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: Describe the first step of the copper-catalysed reaction between peroxodisulfate and thiosulfate ions, including the role of CuвЃє.
original_flashcards.back: Copper(I) ions (CuвЃє) act as a homogeneous catalyst. They react with the strong oxidising agent, peroxodisulfate ions (Sв‚‚Oв‚€ВІвЃ»), to form copper(II) ions (CuВІвЃє) and sulfate ions (SOв‚„ВІвЃ»).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: Write the balanced ionic equation for the first step of the copper-catalysed reaction where CuвЃє is oxidised by peroxodisulfate.
original_flashcards.back: Equation 1: 2CuвЃє(aq) + Sв‚‚Oв‚€ВІвЃ»(aq) в†’ 2CuВІвЃє(aq) + 2SOв‚„ВІвЃ»(aq)

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.subtopic: Homogeneous Catalysis, Redox Reactions, Catalyst Regeneration
original_flashcards.front: Describe the second step of the copper-catalysed reaction, including catalyst regeneration and the overall effect on reaction rate. Write its equation.
original_flashcards.back:
The copper(II) ions (CuВІвЃє) formed in the first step then react with thiosulfate ions (Sв‚‚Oв‚ѓВІвЃ») to regenerate copper(I) ions (CuвЃє) and form tetrathionate ions (Sв‚„Oв‚†ВІвЃ»).
Equation 2: 2CuВІвЃє(aq) + 2Sв‚‚Oв‚ѓВІвЃ»(aq) в†’ 2CuвЃє(aq) + Sв‚„Oв‚†ВІвЃ»(aq)
This two-step pathway has lower activation energies than the direct reaction, speeding up the overall process.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: Explain why the uncatalysed reaction between permanganate (MnOв‚„вЃ») and oxalate (Cв‚‚Oв‚„ВІвЃ») ions is initially slow.
original_flashcards.back: Initially, the reaction is slow because both permanganate (MnOв‚„вЃ») and oxalate (Cв‚‚Oв‚„ВІвЃ») are negatively charged ions. They repel each other, leading to a high activation energy for the direct reaction.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: Describe the first step of the autocatalytic mechanism involving MnВІвЃє ions in the reaction between MnOв‚„вЃ» and Cв‚‚Oв‚„ВІвЃ», including the balanced equation.
original_flashcards.back:
MnВІвЃє ions act as a homogeneous catalyst. They react with MnOв‚„вЃ» ions (permanganate) to form MnВівЃє ions. This is a redox reaction where MnВІвЃє is oxidised to MnВівЃє and MnOв‚„вЃ» is reduced.
Equation 1: MnOв‚„вЃ» + 8HвЃє + 4MnВІвЃє в†’ 5MnВівЃє + 4Hв‚‚O

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: Describe the second step of the autocatalytic mechanism for the MnOв‚„вЃ»/Cв‚‚Oв‚„ВІвЃ» reaction, explaining catalyst regeneration and the overall effect on reaction rate. Include the balanced equation.
original_flashcards.back:
The MnВівЃє ions formed then oxidise the Cв‚‚Oв‚„ВІвЃ» ions (oxalate) back to MnВІвЃє ions, regenerating the catalyst. This two-step catalytic pathway has a lower activation energy than the direct reaction, thus speeding up the overall reaction. As more MnВІвЃє is formed, the catalytic cycle becomes faster, leading to the observed increase in reaction rate.
Equation 2: 2MnВівЃє + Cв‚‚Oв‚„ВІвЃ» в†’ 2MnВІвЃє + 2COв‚‚

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.subtopic: Maxwell-boltzmann Distribution, Activation Energy, Temperature Effects
original_flashcards.front: Consider a Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution curve where the shaded area represents molecules with energy equal to or greater than the activation energy. What happens to this shaded area if the temperature of the system is increased?
original_flashcards.back: If the temperature of the system is increased, the shaded area (representing molecules with energy в‰Ґ activation energy) would increase.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Explain why an increase in the shaded area of a Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution (representing molecules with E в‰Ґ Ea) leads to an increased reaction rate.
original_flashcards.back: An increase in temperature shifts the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution curve to the right, meaning a greater proportion of molecules possess energy equal to or greater than the activation energy (Ea). This leads to more successful collisions per unit time and thus an increased reaction rate.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front:
Which statement correctly describes a characteristic of a transition metal catalyst?
A. They are always in the same physical state as the reactants.
B. They increase the activation energy of a reaction.
C. They are consumed during the reaction.
D. They provide an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy.
original_flashcards.back: D. They provide an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: What is the primary function of a catalyst in a chemical reaction?
original_flashcards.back: A catalyst speeds up the rate of a chemical reaction by providing an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy, without being chemically consumed in the overall process.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Explain why a finely divided catalyst or one with a large surface area is more effective in increasing the rate of reaction.
original_flashcards.back: A finely divided catalyst or a catalyst with a large surface area (e.g., on a support material) increases the number of available active sites for the reaction.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: How does an increased number of active sites on a catalyst lead to a faster reaction rate?
original_flashcards.back: An increased number of active sites allows more reactant molecules to adsorb onto the catalyst surface and react simultaneously. This increases the frequency of successful collisions between adsorbed reactants, thus increasing the rate of reaction.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front:
Which statement about catalysts is correct?
A. Catalysts are always involved in the rate-determining step.
B. Catalysts change the position of equilibrium.
C. Catalysts increase the yield of products.
D. Catalysts remain chemically unchanged at the end of the reaction.
original_flashcards.back: D. Catalysts remain chemically unchanged at the end of the reaction.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What is a key defining characteristic of a catalyst in terms of its chemical state after a reaction?
original_flashcards.back: A key defining characteristic of a catalyst is that it remains chemically unchanged at the end of the reaction, even though it participates in the reaction mechanism.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 4: Inorganic Chemistry and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: Why is a water bath often used for heating organic reactions involving volatile compounds?
original_flashcards.back: A water bath provides even heating and helps prevent overheating, which is crucial for volatile compounds.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: State another reason why a water bath is preferred for heating volatile organic compounds.
original_flashcards.back: It prevents the volatile compound from catching fire and avoids dangerous pressure build-up, allowing accurate temperature control below its boiling point.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Why is one reactant often kept in large excess when determining the order of reaction with respect to another reactant?
original_flashcards.back: Keeping one reactant (e.g., B) in large excess ensures that its concentration remains essentially constant throughout the reaction.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 4: Inorganic Chemistry and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: What is the implication of keeping a reactant in large excess on the rate equation?
original_flashcards.back: It simplifies the rate equation, making the reaction effectively depend only on the concentration of the other reactant (a 'pseudo-order' reaction).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: When plotting a graph to determine the order of reaction with respect to hydrogen peroxide, what should be plotted on the x-axis and y-axis?
original_flashcards.back: Plot the initial concentration of hydrogen peroxide on the x-axis (with correct units) and the initial rate of reaction on the y-axis (with correct units).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What are the key requirements for selecting scales and plotting points accurately on a kinetics graph?
original_flashcards.back: Use appropriate scales that cover at least half of the graph paper, and plot all experimental points accurately.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Once points are plotted on a kinetics graph, what type of line should be drawn?
original_flashcards.back: Draw a smooth curve or a straight line of best fit through the plotted points.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What is the primary role of dilute sulfuric acid in a manganate(VII) titration?
original_flashcards.back: Dilute sulfuric acid provides the HвЃє ions necessary for the reduction of manganate(VII) ions (MnOв‚„вЃ») to manganese(II) ions (MnВІвЃє).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Besides providing HвЃє ions, why is dilute sulfuric acid crucial in manganate(VII) titrations?
original_flashcards.back: It ensures that the manganate(VII) is exclusively reduced to MnВІвЃє. In neutral or alkaline conditions, it would be reduced to other oxidation states like MnOв‚‚ (manganese(IV) oxide), complicating the reaction stoichiometry.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: How does increasing the temperature affect the kinetic energy of reactant molecules?
original_flashcards.back: Increasing the temperature increases the average kinetic energy of the molecules.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: Explain how increased kinetic energy due to higher temperature leads to an increased rate constant.
original_flashcards.back: A higher average kinetic energy means a greater proportion of molecules possess energy equal to or greater than the activation energy. This results in more frequent and effective collisions, thereby increasing the rate constant.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: How do impurities, like sulfur compounds, initially affect a catalyst's active sites?
original_flashcards.back: Impurities, such as sulfur compounds, can adsorb strongly onto the active sites of the catalyst.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What is the consequence of impurities adsorbing onto a catalyst's active sites?
original_flashcards.back: This prevents the reactant molecules (e.g., nitrogen and hydrogen in the Haber process) from adsorbing onto these sites, effectively reducing the available surface area for catalysis.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What happens if impurities form strong bonds with a catalyst?
original_flashcards.back: If these impurities form strong bonds, they may not desorb easily, leading to permanent poisoning and deactivation of the catalyst.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What quantity is typically represented on the x-axis of a Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution curve?
original_flashcards.back: Energy (E) or Molecular energy.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: What specific type of energy is most commonly associated with the x-axis of a Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution?
original_flashcards.back: Kinetic energy.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: How does increasing the temperature affect the peak of a Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution curve?
original_flashcards.back: The peak of the curve shifts to the right (indicating higher average energy) and becomes lower (as the distribution spreads out).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: Besides the peak, what other changes are observed in a Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution curve when the temperature increases?
original_flashcards.back: The curve becomes broader, and the 'tail' extends further to the right, indicating a greater proportion of molecules with energies equal to or exceeding the activation energy.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: How does increasing temperature affect the average kinetic energy of reacting particles?
original_flashcards.back: Increasing the temperature increases the average kinetic energy of the reactant molecules/particles.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Explain how the change in kinetic energy at higher temperatures leads to a faster reaction rate, considering collision theory.
original_flashcards.back: At higher temperatures, molecules move faster, leading to more frequent collisions. Crucially, a significantly greater proportion of these collisions will possess energy equal to or greater than the activation energy (Ea), resulting in more successful collisions and thus a faster reaction rate.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What is the primary role of a catalyst in a chemical reaction, specifically concerning activation energy?
original_flashcards.back: A catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with a *lower activation energy* (Ecat) compared to the uncatalysed reaction (Ea).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Explain how lowering the activation energy through catalysis leads to an increased reaction rate.
original_flashcards.back: By lowering the activation energy, a greater proportion of reactant particles possess energy equal to or greater than this new, lower activation energy. This leads to more successful collisions per unit time, thereby increasing the reaction rate.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front:
Which of the following statements is **true** regarding alkanes?
A) They are saturated hydrocarbons.
B) They contain at least one carbon-carbon double bond.
C) They readily undergo addition reactions.
D) Their general formula is CnH2n.
original_flashcards.back: A) They are saturated hydrocarbons. Alkanes only contain carbon-carbon single bonds and are saturated with hydrogen atoms.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What defines a saturated hydrocarbon?
original_flashcards.back: A saturated hydrocarbon is a hydrocarbon containing only carbon-carbon single bonds and carbon-hydrogen single bonds, meaning it contains the maximum possible number of hydrogen atoms for its carbon skeleton.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Consider a reaction where 2 moles of gaseous reactants form 5 moles of gaseous products in a sealed container. How does the total number of gas moles change during this reaction?
original_flashcards.back: The total number of moles of gas *increases* during the reaction, from 2 moles of reactant gas to 5 moles of product gas.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Based on the change in the number of gas moles (2 reactants в†’ 5 products), what effect would this reaction have on the pressure inside a sealed container at constant temperature?
original_flashcards.back: Since the number of moles of gas increases, the pressure inside the sealed container will *increase* (assuming constant temperature and volume), according to the ideal gas law (PV=nRT), where P is directly proportional to n.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: How do you calculate the relative atomic mass of an element given the mass and abundance of its isotopes?
original_flashcards.back:
To calculate the relative atomic mass (Ar), multiply the mass of each isotope by its fractional abundance, and then sum these values:
Ar = (mass of isotope 1 Г— fractional abundance 1) + (mass of isotope 2 Г— fractional abundance 2) + ...

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: Calculate the relative atomic mass of gallium to two decimal places, given its isotopes: вЃ¶вЃ№Ga (60.11%) and вЃ·В№Ga (39.89%).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: What is the full electron configuration for a neutral vanadium atom (V)?
original_flashcards.back:
Vanadium (V) has an atomic number of 23.
Full electron configuration: 1sВІ2sВІ2pвЃ¶3sВІ3pвЃ¶4sВІ3dВі

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: Write the full electron configuration for a vanadium(III) ion, VВівЃє.
original_flashcards.back:
For transition metal ions, electrons are removed from the 4s subshell before the 3d subshell.
Neutral V: 1sВІ2sВІ2pвЃ¶3sВІ3pвЃ¶4sВІ3dВі
To form VВівЃє, remove 2 electrons from 4s and 1 electron from 3d.
Full electron configuration for VВівЃє: 1sВІ2sВІ2pвЃ¶3sВІ3pвЃ¶3dВІ

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table
board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: From which orbital is the first electron removed from a sulfur atom, and what is its occupancy?
original_flashcards.back: Sulfur's electron configuration is [Ne]3sВІ3pвЃґ. The first electron removed comes from a paired orbital within the 3p subshell.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table
board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: How does electron-electron repulsion within an orbital affect the energy required to remove an electron?
original_flashcards.back: When two electrons occupy the same orbital, they experience electron-electron repulsion. This repulsion destabilises the orbital and makes it easier to remove one of the paired electrons, thus lowering the ionisation energy.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table
board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: Explain why the first ionisation energy of sulfur is lower than that of phosphorus.
original_flashcards.back:
Phosphorus has a 3pВі configuration (three unpaired electrons in 3p orbitals). Sulfur has a 3pвЃґ configuration (one 3p orbital contains a pair of electrons).
The first electron removed from sulfur is one of this paired set. The electron-electron repulsion in this paired orbital makes it easier to remove this electron compared to an unpaired electron in phosphorus, despite sulfur having a greater nuclear charge. This results in a lower first ionisation energy for sulfur.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: The first five successive ionisation energies (kJ molвЃ»В№) for an element X are 738, 1451, 7733, 10540, 13630. Between which two ionisation energies is there the largest jump?
original_flashcards.back: The largest increase in ionisation energy occurs between the second (1451 kJ molвЃ»В№) and the third (7733 kJ molвЃ»В№) ionisation energies.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: What does a significant jump in successive ionisation energies indicate about the electron being removed?
original_flashcards.back: A very large increase in successive ionisation energy indicates that the electron being removed is now coming from an inner electron shell. These electrons are closer to the nucleus and experience less shielding, requiring significantly more energy to remove.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 4: Inorganic Chemistry and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: Deduce the group number of element X, given its successive ionisation energies (738, 1451, 7733, 10540, 13630 kJ molвЃ»В№) and the significant jump after the second electron.
original_flashcards.back:
Since the very large jump in ionisation energy occurs between the second and third electrons removed, it indicates that the first two electrons were valence electrons from the outermost shell.
Therefore, element X has two electrons in its outermost shell and is in Group 2 of the Periodic Table.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: What interaction causes energy to be released when a neutral chlorine atom gains an electron?
original_flashcards.back: The incoming electron is attracted by the positive nucleus of the neutral chlorine atom.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: Explain why the first electron affinity of chlorine is a negative value.
original_flashcards.back:
The first electron affinity is the energy change when one mole of gaseous atoms gains one mole of electrons to form one mole of gaseous 1- ions.
For chlorine, the incoming electron is attracted by the positive nucleus, leading to the release of energy. Since energy is released, the process is exothermic, and thus the first electron affinity value is negative.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: Compare the number of electron shells in a potassium atom (K) and a sodium atom (Na).
original_flashcards.back:
Potassium (K) is in Period 4 and has 4 electron shells.
Sodium (Na) is in Period 3 and has 3 electron shells.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: How does the number of electron shells influence the atomic radius?
original_flashcards.back: As the number of electron shells increases, the outermost electrons are located further away from the nucleus, leading to a larger atomic radius.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: Explain the role of electron shielding in the difference in atomic radii between potassium and sodium.
original_flashcards.back: Potassium has an additional inner electron shell compared to sodium. This extra shell provides greater shielding to the outermost electrons from the positive nuclear charge. This increased shielding reduces the effective nuclear attraction on the valence electrons, allowing them to be held further away and resulting in a larger atomic radius for potassium.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Describe and explain the Ionisation stage in a mass spectrometer.
original_flashcards.back:
**Description:** The sample is vaporised and then ionised, typically by electron impact (for elements/simple molecules) or electrospray (for larger molecules), to form positive ions (cations).
**Explanation:** Only charged particles (ions) can be accelerated by an electric field and deflected by a magnetic field in subsequent stages.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Describe and explain the Acceleration stage in a mass spectrometer.
original_flashcards.back:
**Description:** The positive ions are accelerated by an electric field (e.g., using charged plates) to give them all the same kinetic energy.
**Explanation:** Ensuring all ions have the same kinetic energy allows their velocity to depend solely on their mass (lighter ions move faster), which is crucial for their separation in the next stage.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Describe and explain the Deflection stage in a mass spectrometer.
original_flashcards.back:
**Description:** The accelerated ions pass through a magnetic field, which deflects them. The amount of deflection depends on the ion's mass-to-charge ratio (m/z).
**Explanation:** Lighter ions and/or more highly charged ions are deflected more. This separates the ions based on their m/z, allowing different isotopes or fragments to be distinguished.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Describe and explain the Detection stage in a mass spectrometer.
original_flashcards.back:
**Description:** The deflected ions hit a detector, which measures the arrival of ions. When an ion hits the detector, it generates an electric current/signal proportional to the number of ions arriving.
**Explanation:** This signal allows the abundance of each specific ion (and thus isotope) to be determined. A mass spectrum is generated, showing m/z values versus relative abundance, from which the relative atomic mass can be calculated.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: How do you calculate the equilibrium moles for all species in the PClв‚… decomposition reaction: PClв‚…(g) в‡Њ PClв‚ѓ(g) + Clв‚‚(g), given 2.50 mol of PClв‚… initially and 0.80 mol of Clв‚‚ formed at equilibrium?
original_flashcards.back:
Initial moles:
PClв‚… = 2.50 mol
PClв‚ѓ = 0 mol
Clв‚‚ = 0 mol

Change in moles (based on 0.80 mol Clв‚‚ formed and 1:1:1 stoichiometry):
PClв‚… = -0.80 mol
PClв‚ѓ = +0.80 mol
Clв‚‚ = +0.80 mol

Equilibrium moles:
PClв‚… = 2.50 - 0.80 = 1.70 mol
PClв‚ѓ = 0.80 mol
Clв‚‚ = 0.80 mol

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Given the equilibrium moles of PClв‚… = 1.70 mol, PClв‚ѓ = 0.80 mol, and Clв‚‚ = 0.80 mol in a 5.0 dmВі container, calculate the equilibrium concentrations for each species.
original_flashcards.back:
Equilibrium concentrations (Volume = 5.0 dmВі):
[PClв‚…] = 1.70 mol / 5.0 dmВі = 0.34 mol dmвЃ»Ві
[PClв‚ѓ] = 0.80 mol / 5.0 dmВі = 0.16 mol dmвЃ»Ві
[Clв‚‚] = 0.80 mol / 5.0 dmВі = 0.16 mol dmвЃ»Ві

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: State the equilibrium constant expression (Kc) for the reaction: PClв‚…(g) в‡Њ PClв‚ѓ(g) + Clв‚‚(g), and substitute the equilibrium concentrations: [PClв‚…]=0.34, [PClв‚ѓ]=0.16, [Clв‚‚]=0.16.
original_flashcards.back:
Kc expression: Kc = [PClв‚ѓ][Clв‚‚] / [PClв‚…]
Substitution: Kc = (0.16)(0.16) / (0.34)

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Calculate the value of Kc from Kc = (0.16)(0.16) / (0.34) and state its units, giving your answer to an appropriate number of significant figures.
original_flashcards.back:
Kc = 0.0256 / 0.34 = 0.07529...
Kc = 0.0753 (to 3 significant figures, as the concentrations are given to 2 or 3 sig figs)
Units: (mol dmвЃ»Ві)(mol dmвЃ»Ві) / (mol dmвЃ»Ві) = mol dmвЃ»Ві

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: According to Le Chatelier's Principle, how does an increase in temperature affect the position of equilibrium for a reversible reaction?
original_flashcards.back: An increase in temperature will cause the equilibrium to shift in the direction of the endothermic reaction. This shift attempts to absorb the added heat and counteract the change in temperature.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: For the ammonia synthesis (Nв‚‚(g) + 3Hв‚‚(g) в‡Њ 2NHв‚ѓ(g), О”H = -92 kJ molвЃ»В№), explain how heating the equilibrium mixture from 400 В°C to 550 В°C affects the equilibrium position.
original_flashcards.back: The forward reaction is exothermic (О”H = -92 kJ molвЃ»В№), meaning the reverse reaction (decomposition of NHв‚ѓ) is endothermic. An increase in temperature favours the endothermic direction. Therefore, the equilibrium will shift to the left, favouring the formation of reactants (Nв‚‚ and Hв‚‚) and decreasing the yield of ammonia.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: How does the shift in equilibrium caused by an increase in temperature (as described for ammonia synthesis) affect the value of the equilibrium constant, Kc?
original_flashcards.back: When the equilibrium shifts to the left (favouring reactants) due to increased temperature, the concentration of products ([NHв‚ѓ]) decreases, and the concentrations of reactants ([Nв‚‚] and [Hв‚‚]) increase. Since Kc = [NHв‚ѓ]ВІ / ([Nв‚‚][Hв‚‚]Ві), a decrease in product concentration and an increase in reactant concentration will result in a decrease in the value of Kc.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: How do you calculate the total moles and mole fractions of Nв‚‚Oв‚„ and NOв‚‚ at equilibrium given 0.60 mol Nв‚‚Oв‚„ and 0.90 mol NOв‚‚?
original_flashcards.back:
1. **Total moles** = 0.60 mol (Nв‚‚Oв‚„) + 0.90 mol (NOв‚‚) = 1.50 mol
2. **Mole fraction Nв‚‚Oв‚„ (X_Nв‚‚Oв‚„)** = 0.60 / 1.50 = 0.40
3. **Mole fraction NOв‚‚ (X_NOв‚‚)** = 0.90 / 1.50 = 0.60

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Given a total pressure of 1.80 atm and mole fractions X_Nв‚‚Oв‚„ = 0.40, X_NOв‚‚ = 0.60, calculate the partial pressures of Nв‚‚Oв‚„ and NOв‚‚.
original_flashcards.back:
1. **Partial pressure Nв‚‚Oв‚„ (p(Nв‚‚Oв‚„))** = X_Nв‚‚Oв‚„ Г— P_total = 0.40 Г— 1.80 atm = 0.72 atm
2. **Partial pressure NOв‚‚ (p(NOв‚‚))** = X_NOв‚‚ Г— P_total = 0.60 Г— 1.80 atm = 1.08 atm

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Write the expression for the equilibrium constant Kp for the reaction: Nв‚‚Oв‚„(g) в‡Њ 2NOв‚‚(g).
original_flashcards.back: Kp = (p(NOв‚‚))ВІ / p(Nв‚‚Oв‚„)

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Calculate the value of Kp for the reaction Nв‚‚Oв‚„(g) в‡Њ 2NOв‚‚(g) given p(Nв‚‚Oв‚„) = 0.72 atm and p(NOв‚‚) = 1.08 atm. Include units.
original_flashcards.back:
Kp = (p(NOв‚‚))ВІ / p(Nв‚‚Oв‚„)
Kp = (1.08 atm)ВІ / 0.72 atm
Kp = 1.1664 atmВІ / 0.72 atm
**Kp = 1.62 atm**

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Calculate the moles of propanoic acid (CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOH) and sodium propanoate (CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COONa) in a buffer solution prepared by mixing 30.0 cmВі of 0.600 mol dmвЃ»Ві propanoic acid with 40.0 cmВі of 0.450 mol dmвЃ»Ві sodium propanoate.
original_flashcards.back:
1. **Moles of CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOH** = (30.0/1000) dmВі Г— 0.600 mol dmвЃ»Ві = 0.0180 mol
2. **Moles of CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COONa** = (40.0/1000) dmВі Г— 0.450 mol dmвЃ»Ві = 0.0180 mol

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Given 0.0180 mol of propanoic acid and 0.0180 mol of propanoate ions in a total volume of 70.0 cmВі, and Ka = 1.34 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ mol dmвЃ»Ві for propanoic acid, calculate the pH of the buffer solution.
original_flashcards.back:
1. **Total volume** = 30.0 cmВі + 40.0 cmВі = 70.0 cmВі = 0.0700 dmВі
2. **[CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOH]** = 0.0180 mol / 0.0700 dmВі = 0.2571 mol dmвЃ»Ві
3. **[CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOвЃ»]** = 0.0180 mol / 0.0700 dmВі = 0.2571 mol dmвЃ»Ві
4. Since [acid] = [salt], the [HвЃє] = Ka = 1.34 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ mol dmвЃ»Ві
5. **pH** = -log[HвЃє] = -$\log(1.34 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ)$ = **4.87**
*(Alternatively, using Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = pKa + $\log([salt]/[acid])$. Since [salt]=[acid], pH = pKa. pKa = -$\log(1.34 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ)$ = 4.87)*

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Explain the initial reaction that occurs when a small amount of strong alkali (OHвЃ») is added to a propanoic acid/propanoate buffer solution.
original_flashcards.back:
When a small amount of strong alkali (OHвЃ») is added, it reacts with the weak acid component of the buffer, propanoic acid (CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOH).
**Equation:** CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOH + OHвЃ» в†’ CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOвЃ» + Hв‚‚O

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Following the reaction with added alkali, explain how the propanoic acid/propanoate buffer solution resists a significant change in pH.
original_flashcards.back:
The reaction CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOH + OHвЃ» в†’ CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOвЃ» + Hв‚‚O removes most of the added hydroxide ions, preventing a significant increase in pH.
The equilibrium CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOH в‡Њ HвЃє + CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOвЃ» shifts to the right to replenish HвЃє ions (or the large reservoir of weak acid is available to react with the added base), thereby maintaining the pH of the solution.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: How do you calculate the initial moles of propanoic acid (CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOH) and propanoate ions (CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOвЃ») when preparing this buffer solution?
original_flashcards.back:
To calculate the initial moles:

Moles of CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOH = Concentration Г— Volume
= 0.250 mol dmвЃ»Ві Г— (75.0/1000) dmВі
= 0.01875 mol

Moles of CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOвЃ» (from CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COONa) = Concentration Г— Volume
= 0.350 mol dmвЃ»Ві Г— (50.0/1000) dmВі
= 0.0175 mol

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Given the moles of propanoic acid and propanoate ions, and the total volume, how do you determine their concentrations and set up the Kв‚ђ expression to find [HвЃє]?
original_flashcards.back:
First, calculate the total volume and concentrations:
Total volume = 75.0 cmВі + 50.0 cmВі = 125.0 cmВі = 0.125 dmВі
[CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOH] = 0.01875 mol / 0.125 dmВі = 0.150 mol dmвЃ»Ві
[CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOвЃ»] = 0.0175 mol / 0.125 dmВі = 0.140 mol dmвЃ»Ві

The Kв‚ђ expression for propanoic acid is: Kв‚ђ = [HвЃє][CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOвЃ»] / [CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOH]

Rearranging to find [HвЃє]:
[HвЃє] = Kв‚ђ Г— ([CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOH] / [CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOвЃ»])

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Using Kв‚ђ = 1.3 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ mol dmвЃ»Ві and the calculated concentrations, calculate [HвЃє] and the pH of the buffer solution.
original_flashcards.back:
Using the rearranged Kв‚ђ expression:
[HвЃє] = Kв‚ђ Г— ([CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOH] / [CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COOвЃ»])
[HвЃє] = (1.3 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ) Г— (0.150 / 0.140)
[HвЃє] = (1.3 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ) Г— 1.0714...
[HвЃє] = 1.3928 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ mol dmвЃ»Ві

Now, calculate pH:
pH = -log[HвЃє]
pH = -$\log(1.3928 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ)$
pH = 4.856 в‰€ 4.86 (to 2 decimal places)

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Using the general equilibrium HIn в‡Њ HвЃє + InвЃ», explain why acid-base indicators change colour.
original_flashcards.back: Acid-base indicators are weak acids or bases where the undissociated form (HIn) and its conjugate base form (InвЃ») have different colours. The equilibrium shifts depending on the concentration of HвЃє ions (pH) in the solution. When HIn is dominant, one colour is observed; when InвЃ» is dominant, a different colour is observed.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: How does an indicator like methyl red show a sharp colour change at the endpoint of a titration?
original_flashcards.back: At the equivalence point of a titration, a very small addition of acid or base causes a large, rapid change in the pH of the solution. This significant change in [HвЃє] causes a substantial shift in the indicator's equilibrium (HIn в‡Њ HвЃє + InвЃ»), leading to a rapid change in the ratio of [HIn] to [InвЃ»]. As the dominant species changes, a sharp and visible colour change occurs.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Given methyl red's pKбµўв‚™ of 5.0, what type of titration is it suitable for, and why?
original_flashcards.back:
Methyl red is suitable for a **strong acid-weak base titration**.

**Justification:** An indicator's colour change range is typically pKбµўв‚™ $\pm$ 1, so for methyl red (pKбµўв‚™ = 5.0), the colour change occurs approximately between pH 4 and pH 6. The equivalence point of a strong acid-weak base titration occurs in the acidic region (typically pH 4-6), making methyl red's colour change range match the steep part of the titration curve at the equivalence point.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What principle is used to calculate lattice energy from other enthalpy changes in a Born-Haber cycle?
original_flashcards.back: Hess's Law. It states that the total enthalpy change for a reaction is independent of the route taken, as long as the initial and final conditions are the same. For a Born-Haber cycle, the standard enthalpy change of formation (О”fHВ°) is equal to the sum of all other enthalpy changes in the cycle.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: List the enthalpy changes (and their stoichiometry) required to construct a Born-Haber cycle for the formation of an ionic compound like CaBrв‚‚(s) from its elements.
original_flashcards.back:
The enthalpy changes involved are:
1. Standard enthalpy change of atomisation of the metal (Ca(s) в†’ Ca(g))
2. First ionisation energy of the metal (Ca(g) в†’ CaвЃє(g) + eвЃ»)
3. Second ionisation energy of the metal (CaвЃє(g) в†’ CaВІвЃє(g) + eвЃ»)
4. Standard enthalpy change of atomisation of the non-metal (Brв‚‚(l) в†’ 2Br(g) вЂ“ note: two moles of Br atoms are needed)
5. First electron affinity of the non-metal (Br(g) + eвЃ» в†’ BrвЃ»(g) вЂ“ note: two moles of BrвЃ» ions are needed)
6. Lattice energy (CaВІвЃє(g) + 2BrвЃ»(g) в†’ CaBrв‚‚(s))
7. Standard enthalpy change of formation of the ionic compound (Ca(s) + Brв‚‚(l) в†’ CaBrв‚‚(s))

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.subtopic: Born-haber Cycles, Lattice Energy, Hess's Law
original_flashcards.front:
Calculate the lattice energy of CaBrв‚‚(s) using the provided data:
О”fHВ°[CaBrв‚‚(s)] = -675 kJ molвЃ»В№
О”atHВ°[Ca(s)] = +178 kJ molвЃ»В№
О”atHВ°[Brв‚‚(l)] = +112 kJ molвЃ»В№ (per mole of Br atoms)
IEв‚Ѓ[Ca(g)] = +590 kJ molвЃ»В№
IEв‚‚[Ca(g)] = +1145 kJ molвЃ»В№
EAв‚Ѓ[Br(g)] = -325 kJ molвЃ»В№
original_flashcards.back:
Using Hess's Law for the Born-Haber cycle:
О”fHВ° = О”atHВ°[Ca] + IEв‚Ѓ[Ca] + IEв‚‚[Ca] + (2 Г— О”atHВ°[Br]) + (2 Г— EAв‚Ѓ[Br]) + Lattice Energy
-675 = +178 + 590 + 1145 + (2 Г— 112) + (2 Г— -325) + Lattice Energy
-675 = 178 + 590 + 1145 + 224 - 650 + Lattice Energy
-675 = 1487 + Lattice Energy
Lattice Energy = -675 - 1487
Lattice Energy = -2162 kJ molвЃ»В№

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: For a reaction to be thermodynamically feasible, what must be the sign of the Gibbs free energy change (О”G)? State the equation linking О”G, О”H, T, and О”Ssystem.
original_flashcards.back: For a reaction to be thermodynamically feasible, the Gibbs free energy change (О”G) must be negative (О”G < 0). The equation linking these terms is: О”G = О”H вЂ“ TО”Ssystem, where О”H is the enthalpy change, T is the absolute temperature, and О”Ssystem is the entropy change of the system.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: For the thermal decomposition of lead(II) oxide (2PbO(s) в†’ 2Pb(s) + Oв‚‚(g)), what are the expected signs for the enthalpy change (О”H) and the entropy change of the system (О”Ssystem)? Justify your answer.
original_flashcards.back:
1.  **Enthalpy change (О”H):** Expected to be positive (О”H > 0). The decomposition of a stable metal oxide into its elements typically requires energy input to break bonds, making it an endothermic process.
2.  **Entropy change of the system (О”Ssystem):** Expected to be positive (О”Ssystem > 0). The reaction produces a gas (Oв‚‚) from a solid reactant (PbO) and solid product (Pb). The formation of gas leads to a significant increase in disorder and randomness, thus increasing the entropy of the system.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.subtopic: Gibbs Free Energy, Thermodynamic Feasibility, Temperature
original_flashcards.front: How does temperature (T) affect the thermodynamic feasibility of the decomposition of PbO, given that О”H is positive and О”Ssystem is positive? Explain using the О”G equation.
original_flashcards.back:
The Gibbs free energy equation is О”G = О”H вЂ“ TО”Ssystem. For the decomposition of PbO, О”H is positive and О”Ssystem is positive.
*   At **low temperatures (small T)**, the TО”Ssystem term is small. Since О”H is positive and large, О”G will be positive (О”G = positive О”H - small positive TО”Ssystem), meaning the reaction is not thermodynamically feasible.
*   As **temperature (T) increases**, the magnitude of the TО”Ssystem term increases. At a sufficiently high temperature, the TО”Ssystem term will become greater than О”H. When TО”Ssystem > О”H, О”G becomes negative (О”G = positive О”H - large positive TО”Ssystem), making the reaction thermodynamically feasible.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Even if the thermal decomposition of PbO is thermodynamically feasible at high temperatures, why does it often require *very high* temperatures to proceed at a significant rate in practice?
original_flashcards.back: Even if a reaction is thermodynamically feasible (О”G < 0) at a certain temperature, it may not occur at a significant rate in practice due to a high activation energy (Ea). The decomposition of PbO, like many thermal decompositions, has a high activation energy. Therefore, very high temperatures (often significantly above the calculated minimum feasible temperature) are required to provide enough kinetic energy to a sufficient number of reacting particles to overcome this high activation barrier and allow the reaction to proceed at a noticeable rate.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: What role does zinc metal play when added to an acidified solution of vanadium(V) ions (VOв‚‚вЃє), given its standard electrode potential (EВ° = -0.76 V) compared to vanadium species?
original_flashcards.back: Zinc metal acts as a reducing agent because its standard electrode potential (EВ° = -0.76 V) is more negative than all the vanadium reduction potentials listed. This indicates that zinc has a greater tendency to be oxidised, thereby reducing the vanadium species.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: Calculate the Ecell and determine the feasibility for the reduction of VOв‚‚вЃє to VOВІвЃє by zinc. (EВ°(VOв‚‚вЃє/VOВІвЃє) = +1.00 V, EВ°(ZnВІвЃє/Zn) = -0.76 V)
original_flashcards.back:
Ecell = EВ°(VOв‚‚вЃє/VOВІвЃє) - EВ°(ZnВІвЃє/Zn) = (+1.00 V) - (-0.76 V) = +1.76 V.
Since Ecell > 0, this reduction reaction is thermodynamically feasible.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: Calculate the Ecell and determine the feasibility for the further reduction steps of vanadium by zinc: VOВІвЃє to VВівЃє and VВівЃє to VВІвЃє. (EВ°(VOВІвЃє/VВівЃє) = +0.34 V, EВ°(VВівЃє/VВІвЃє) = -0.26 V, EВ°(ZnВІвЃє/Zn) = -0.76 V)
original_flashcards.back:
For VOВІвЃє to VВівЃє: Ecell = EВ°(VOВІвЃє/VВівЃє) - EВ°(ZnВІвЃє/Zn) = (+0.34 V) - (-0.76 V) = +1.10 V. Since Ecell > 0, this reaction is feasible.
For VВівЃє to VВІвЃє: Ecell = EВ°(VВівЃє/VВІвЃє) - EВ°(ZnВІвЃє/Zn) = (-0.26 V) - (-0.76 V) = +0.50 V. Since Ecell > 0, this reaction is feasible.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: Given that all reduction steps of vanadium by excess zinc are feasible, what is the final oxidation state of vanadium formed when excess zinc is added to acidified VOв‚‚вЃє ions?
original_flashcards.back: Since all three reduction steps (VOв‚‚вЃє в†’ VOВІвЃє, VOВІвЃє в†’ VВівЃє, and VВівЃє в†’ VВІвЃє) are thermodynamically feasible with positive Ecell values, the vanadium will be fully reduced to its lowest possible oxidation state in this series. The final oxidation state of vanadium formed is +2 (VВІвЃє).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: List the essential components of a hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell operating under acidic conditions.
original_flashcards.back: Essential components include: two separate compartments (or a proton exchange membrane), porous platinum or carbon electrodes, an acidic electrolyte (e.g., dilute Hв‚‚SOв‚„), inlets for Hв‚‚(g) and Oв‚‚(g), an external circuit connecting the electrodes, and an outlet for Hв‚‚O(l).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: In a hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell operating under acidic conditions, what are the typical electrode materials, and what type of electrolyte is used?
original_flashcards.back: The electrodes are typically made of porous platinum or carbon. The electrolyte is an acidic solution, such as dilute sulfuric acid (Hв‚‚SOв‚„), or a proton exchange membrane (PEM).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: Describe the direction of electron flow in the external circuit of a hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell and state the half-equation occurring at the hydrogen electrode (anode).
original_flashcards.back:
Electrons flow from the hydrogen electrode (anode, where oxidation occurs) to the oxygen electrode (cathode) through the external circuit.
At the hydrogen electrode (anode): Hв‚‚ в†’ 2HвЃє + 2eвЃ»

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: Write the half-equation occurring at the oxygen electrode (cathode) in a hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell operating under acidic conditions.
original_flashcards.back: At the oxygen electrode (cathode, where reduction occurs): Oв‚‚ + 4HвЃє + 4eвЃ» в†’ 2Hв‚‚O

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 4: Inorganic Chemistry and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: What is the initial observation when aqueous sodium hydroxide is added to a solution containing iron(III) ions?
original_flashcards.back: A red-brown (or orange-brown) precipitate forms.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 4: Inorganic Chemistry and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: What is the chemical identity and formula of the precipitate formed when aqueous sodium hydroxide is added to iron(III) ions?
original_flashcards.back: The precipitate is iron(III) hydroxide, with the formula Fe(OH)в‚ѓ.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 4: Inorganic Chemistry and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: What happens to the iron(III) hydroxide precipitate when excess aqueous sodium hydroxide is added?
original_flashcards.back: The iron(III) hydroxide precipitate is insoluble in excess aqueous sodium hydroxide.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.subtopic: Complex Ions, Ligand Exchange, Transition Metal Observations
original_flashcards.front: Describe the observation when concentrated hydrochloric acid is added to a pink solution of [Co(Hв‚‚O)в‚†]ВІвЃє ions.
original_flashcards.back: The solution changes colour from pink to blue. This is due to the formation of the tetrachlorocobaltate(II) ion, [CoClв‚„]ВІвЃ».

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: What characteristic of cobalt(II) ions (and other transition metals) allows them to act as catalysts?
original_flashcards.back: Cobalt is a transition metal, meaning its ions have an incomplete d-subshell (e.g., CoВІвЃє has the electronic configuration [Ar] 3dвЃ·).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table
board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.subtopic: Catalysis, Electronic Configuration, Redox Reactions, Oxidation States
original_flashcards.front: How does the electronic configuration of cobalt(II) ions relate to their ability to act as catalysts in redox reactions?
original_flashcards.back: The incomplete d-subshell allows cobalt to exhibit variable oxidation states (e.g., CoВІвЃє and CoВівЃє).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.subtopic: Catalysis, Redox Reactions, Activation Energy, Reaction Mechanism
original_flashcards.front: Explain the mechanism by which cobalt(II) ions catalyse redox reactions, considering their variable oxidation states.
original_flashcards.back: In catalysis, the cobalt ion can be oxidised by one reactant and subsequently reduced by the other reactant (or vice versa). This provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy, thus speeding up the reaction.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What is the first step to calculate the activation energy (Eв‚ђ) for a reaction using the Arrhenius equation with two sets of temperature and rate constant data?
original_flashcards.back: Identify and list all given values: kв‚Ѓ, kв‚‚, Tв‚Ѓ, Tв‚‚, and the gas constant R. Ensure temperatures are in Kelvin.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: How do you substitute the given values into the Arrhenius equation, $\ln(kв‚‚/kв‚Ѓ)$ = -Eв‚ђ/R (1/Tв‚‚ - 1/Tв‚Ѓ), and begin to simplify it?
original_flashcards.back:
Substitute the identified values into the equation:
$\ln(8.5 Г— 10вЃ»вЃ¶ / 9.7 Г— 10вЃ»вЃ·)$ = -Eв‚ђ / 8.31 ($\frac{1}{600}$ - $\frac{1}{550}$)

Then, calculate the numerical values for the natural logarithm term and the temperature difference term:
$\ln(8.7628866)$ = 2.1706
($\frac{1}{600}$ - $\frac{1}{550}$) = (0.001666... - 0.001818...) = -0.0001515...

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: After simplifying the Arrhenius equation to 2.1706 = -Eв‚ђ / 8.31 (-0.0001515...), how do you rearrange and solve for the activation energy (Eв‚ђ)?
original_flashcards.back:
Rearrange the equation:
2.1706 = Eв‚ђ / 8.31 (0.0001515...)
Eв‚ђ = (2.1706 Г— 8.31) / (0.0001515...)

Calculate the value:
Eв‚ђ = 119100 J molвЃ»В№

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.OCR-A: Module 5: Physical Chemistry and Transition Elements

original_flashcards.front: What are the final steps for stating the activation energy (Eв‚ђ) value, including appropriate units and significant figures?
original_flashcards.back:
Convert the activation energy from J molвЃ»В№ to kJ molвЃ»В№ by dividing by 1000:
Eв‚ђ = 119100 J molвЃ»В№ = +119.1 kJ molвЃ»В№

Round the answer to an appropriate number of significant figures (usually 2 or 3, consistent with the given data):
Eв‚ђ = +119 kJ molвЃ»В№ (3 SF) or +120 kJ molвЃ»В№ (2 SF).

Ensure the units are kJ molвЃ»В№ and the sign is positive.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: How do you determine the order of reaction with respect to Sв‚‚Oв‚€ВІвЃ» from the given initial rate data?
original_flashcards.back:
Compare Experiment 1 and Experiment 2:
[Sв‚‚Oв‚€ВІвЃ»] doubles (0.080 to 0.160 mol dmвЃ»Ві).
[IвЃ»] remains constant (0.040 mol dmвЃ»Ві).
Initial Rate doubles (1.20 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ to 2.40 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ mol dmвЃ»Ві sвЃ»В№).
Since doubling [Sв‚‚Oв‚€ВІвЃ»] doubles the rate, the reaction is first order with respect to Sв‚‚Oв‚€ВІвЃ».

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: How do you determine the order of reaction with respect to IвЃ» and then write the overall rate equation for the reaction?
original_flashcards.back:
Compare Experiment 1 and Experiment 3:
[Sв‚‚Oв‚€ВІвЃ»] remains constant (0.080 mol dmвЃ»Ві).
[IвЃ»] doubles (0.040 to 0.080 mol dmвЃ»Ві).
Initial Rate quadruples (1.20 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ to 4.80 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ mol dmвЃ»Ві sвЃ»В№).
Since doubling [IвЃ»] quadruples the rate (2ВІ), the reaction is second order with respect to IвЃ».

Overall rate equation: Rate = k[Sв‚‚Oв‚€ВІвЃ»][IвЃ»]ВІ

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: How do you calculate the rate constant (k) for this reaction, including its units and appropriate significant figures?
original_flashcards.back:
Use the rate equation (Rate = k[Sв‚‚Oв‚€ВІвЃ»][IвЃ»]ВІ) and data from any experiment (e.g., Experiment 1).
Rearrange to solve for k: k = Rate / ([Sв‚‚Oв‚€ВІвЃ»][IвЃ»]ВІ)

Substitute values from Experiment 1:
k = (1.20 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ mol dmвЃ»Ві sвЃ»В№) / (0.080 mol dmвЃ»Ві Г— (0.040 mol dmвЃ»Ві)ВІ) 
k = (1.20 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ) / (0.080 Г— 0.0016)
k = (1.20 Г— 10вЃ»вЃµ) / (1.28 Г— 10вЃ»вЃґ)
k = 0.09375

Round to 2 or 3 significant figures (consistent with input data):
k = 0.094 molвЃ»ВІ dmвЃ¶ sвЃ»В№ (to 2 SF)

Units for k: (mol dmвЃ»Ві sвЃ»В№) / (mol dmвЃ»Ві Г— (mol dmвЃ»Ві)ВІ) = (mol dmвЃ»Ві sвЃ»В№) / (molВі dmвЃ»вЃ№) = molвЃ»ВІ dmвЃ¶ sвЃ»В№.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: How do you prepare a Grignard reagent from 2-bromopropane?
original_flashcards.back:
Reagents: 2-bromopropane (CHв‚ѓCHBrCHв‚ѓ) and magnesium (Mg).
Conditions: Dry ether (or dry ethoxyethane).
Intermediate formed: Isopropylmagnesium bromide (CHв‚ѓCH(MgBr)CHв‚ѓ).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Which carbonyl compound reacts with isopropylmagnesium bromide to form the intermediate for 2-methylbutan-2-ol, and under what conditions?
original_flashcards.back:
To form 2-methylbutan-2-ol (a tertiary alcohol), isopropylmagnesium bromide (CHв‚ѓCH(MgBr)CHв‚ѓ) reacts with **Butan-2-one** (CHв‚ѓCOCHв‚‚CHв‚ѓ).
Conditions: Dry ether (or dry ethoxyethane).
Intermediate: CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚C(CHв‚ѓ)(OMgBr)CH(CHв‚ѓ)в‚‚ (a magnesium alkoxide).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: How is 2-methylbutan-2-ol formed from its magnesium alkoxide intermediate?
original_flashcards.back:
The magnesium alkoxide intermediate (CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚C(CHв‚ѓ)(OMgBr)CH(CHв‚ѓ)в‚‚) is hydrolysed.
Reagents: Dilute acid (e.g., dilute HCl or Hв‚‚SOв‚„) / HвЃє(aq).
Product: 2-methylbutan-2-ol (CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚C(CHв‚ѓ)(OH)CH(CHв‚ѓ)в‚‚).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Compound P (Cв‚…Hв‚Ѓв‚ЂO) reacts with Tollens' reagent but does not decolourise bromine water. Deduce its structure and justify.
original_flashcards.back:
Structure: **Pentanal** (CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚CHв‚‚CHв‚‚CHO).
Justification: It reacts with Tollens' reagent, indicating the presence of an aldehyde functional group (-CHO). It does not decolourise bromine water, meaning it does not contain a carbon-carbon double bond.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Compound Q (Cв‚…Hв‚Ѓв‚ЂO) does not react with Tollens' reagent but readily decolourises bromine water. Deduce its structure and justify.
original_flashcards.back:
Structure: **Pent-3-en-2-one** (CHв‚ѓCOCH=CHCHв‚ѓ).
Justification: It does not react with Tollens' reagent, indicating it is a ketone (not an aldehyde). It readily decolourises bromine water, indicating the presence of a carbon-carbon double bond (C=C).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What is the key functional group difference and test result distinguishing Compound P (reacts with Tollens') from Compound Q (does not react with Tollens')?
original_flashcards.back: Compound P contains an **aldehyde** group (-CHO), which is oxidised by Tollens' reagent to form a silver mirror. Compound Q contains a **ketone** group (C=O, with carbons on both sides), which cannot be oxidised by Tollens' reagent.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Compound Q is pent-3-en-2-one. Draw the skeletal formula for its E-isomer.
original_flashcards.back:
Skeletal formula for E-pent-3-en-2-one:

   CHв‚ѓ
    \
     C = C
    //     \
   CHв‚ѓCO    H

(The higher priority groups, CHв‚ѓCO and CHв‚ѓ, are on opposite sides of the C=C double bond, indicating the E-isomer.)

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Rank ammonia, ethylamine, and phenylamine in order of increasing basicity.
original_flashcards.back: Phenylamine < Ammonia < Ethylamine.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Explain why ethylamine is a stronger base than ammonia.
original_flashcards.back: Ethylamine is a stronger base than ammonia because the ethyl group is electron-releasing (has a positive inductive effect). This increases the electron density on the nitrogen atom, making the lone pair more available to accept a proton.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Explain why phenylamine is a weaker base than ammonia.
original_flashcards.back: Phenylamine is a weaker base than ammonia because the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom is delocalised into the benzene ring. This reduces the electron density on the nitrogen, making the lone pair less available to accept a proton.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: Outline the two-step synthetic route for preparing N-methylpropanamide starting from propanoic acid, including the intermediate structure.
original_flashcards.back:
Step 1: Propanoic acid в†’ Propanoyl chloride (CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚COCl)
Step 2: Propanoyl chloride в†’ N-methylpropanamide (CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚CONHCHв‚ѓ)

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What are the reagents and conditions for the first step in the synthesis of N-methylpropanamide from propanoic acid?
original_flashcards.back:
The first step is converting propanoic acid to propanoyl chloride.
Reagent: Thionyl chloride (SOClв‚‚) OR Phosphorus pentachloride (PClв‚…) OR Phosphorus trichloride (PClв‚ѓ)
Conditions: Heat / Reflux

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: What are the reagents and conditions for the second step in the synthesis of N-methylpropanamide from propanoic acid?
original_flashcards.back:
The second step is converting propanoyl chloride to N-methylpropanamide.
Reagent: Methylamine (CHв‚ѓNHв‚‚)
Conditions: Room temperature
(This is a nucleophilic addition-elimination reaction.)

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry

board_topics.Edexcel: Topic 1: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

original_flashcards.front: Compound R has molecular formula Cв‚†Hв‚Ѓв‚‚Oв‚‚. Given its В№H NMR data (Оґ 4.15 ppm quartet 2H, Оґ 2.50 ppm septet 1H, Оґ 1.25 ppm triplet 3H, Оґ 1.18 ppm doublet 6H), deduce its full displayed formula.
original_flashcards.back:
The compound is Ethyl 2-methylpropanoate.
Displayed Formula (Skeletal representation for clarity):
CHв‚ѓCHв‚‚OOCCH(CHв‚ѓ)в‚‚

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: For compound R (Ethyl 2-methylpropanoate), justify the В№H NMR peak at Оґ 4.15 ppm (quartet, 2H).
original_flashcards.back: This peak corresponds to the -O-CHв‚‚- group of the ethyl ester. The 2 protons are adjacent to a CHв‚ѓ group, causing a quartet (N+1 rule). The chemical shift is characteristic of protons on a carbon directly bonded to an oxygen atom.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: For compound R (Ethyl 2-methylpropanoate), justify the В№H NMR peak at Оґ 2.50 ppm (septet, 1H).
original_flashcards.back: This peak corresponds to the -CH- proton in the (CHв‚ѓ)в‚‚CH- part. This proton is adjacent to six equivalent protons (from the two CHв‚ѓ groups), causing a septet (6+1 rule). The chemical shift is characteristic of a proton on a carbon adjacent to a carbonyl group and branched alkyl groups.

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
original_flashcards.front: For compound R (Ethyl 2-methylpropanoate), justify the В№H NMR peak at Оґ 1.25 ppm (triplet, 3H).
original_flashcards.back: This peak corresponds to the -CHв‚ѓ group of the ethyl ester (-O-CHв‚‚CHв‚ѓ). The 3 protons are adjacent to a CHв‚‚ group, causing a triplet (N+1 rule).

original_flashcards.subject: Chemistry
